title: Cross-Cutting Redundancy Intelligence Briefing
scenarioTrigger: User indicates they are making redundancies, planning workforce reductions, restructuring roles, or selecting employees for redundancy. Also triggered by queries about redundancy payments, consultation obligations, or redundancy during TUPE transfers.
briefingIntroduction: Redundancy in UK employment law is not governed by a single instrument. It sits at the intersection of at least seven major legislative frameworks, each imposing distinct obligations on the employer at different stages of the process. An employer making redundancies must simultaneously comply with: the statutory definition and payment framework (ERA 1996 Part XI), the fairness requirements for dismissal (ERA 1996 Part X), collective consultation obligations (TULRCA 1992 s.188–198), anti-discrimination requirements (EqA 2010), enhanced protections for pregnant employees and those on family leave (MPL 1999 and the Protection from Redundancy Act 2023), additional protections in business transfer contexts (TUPE 2006 Reg.7), and forthcoming strengthened protections under ERA 2025 (not yet in force). Eileen assembles these into a single coherent briefing based on the employer's specific circumstances.

sections.title: Statutory definition of redundancy
sections.summary: An employee is dismissed by reason of redundancy where the dismissal is wholly or mainly attributable to: (a) the employer ceasing, or intending to cease, to carry on the business at all or at the place where the employee is employed; or (b) the requirements of the business for employees to carry out work of a particular kind having ceased or diminished or being expected to cease or diminish.
sections.text:
Section 139(1) of the Employment Rights Act 1996 defines redundancy. An employee who is dismissed shall be taken to be dismissed by reason of redundancy if the dismissal is wholly or mainly attributable to—

(a) the fact that his employer has ceased or intends to cease—
(i) to carry on the business for the purposes of which the employee was employed by him, or
(ii) to carry on that business in the place where the employee was so employed, or

(b) the fact that the requirements of that business—
(i) for employees to carry out work of a particular kind, or
(ii) for employees to carry out work of a particular kind in the place where the employee was employed by the employer,
have ceased or diminished or are expected to cease or diminish.
sections.keyPrinciple: The statutory definition focuses on the employer's business needs, not the employee's role title. The test is whether the requirement for employees to carry out work of a particular kind has ceased or diminished.
refs: ERA 1996 s.139 (definition of redundancy)
ERA 1996 s.135 (right to redundancy payment)
ERA 1996 s.155 (qualifying period — 2 years' continuous employment required for statutory redundancy payment)
Safeway Stores plc v Burrell [1997] ICR 523 (EAT) — three-stage test
Murray v Foyle Meats Ltd [2000] 1 AC 51 (UKHL) — 'work of a particular kind' test

leadingCases.name: Murray v Foyle Meats Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The redundancy definition in s.139 requires a simple causation test: was the dismissal attributable to a diminution in the employer's requirements for employees to carry out work of a particular kind?
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that both limbs of s.139(1)(b) require a simple question of causation. The tribunal must ask whether the dismissal was attributable to a diminution in the employer's need for employees to do work of a particular kind. There is no requirement that the employee was employed to do work of that particular kind — only that the employer's overall requirements had diminished.
leadingCases.significance: The leading UKHL authority on the statutory definition of redundancy. Rejected the 'contract test' and 'function test' in favour of a simple causation analysis. Applied and followed in virtually all subsequent redundancy cases.

leadingCases.name: Safeway Stores plc v Burrell
leadingCases.principle: The redundancy analysis follows a three-stage test: (1) was the employee dismissed? (2) had the requirements of the business for employees to carry out work of a particular kind ceased or diminished? (3) was the dismissal caused wholly or mainly by that state of affairs?
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT established a structured three-stage approach to determining whether a dismissal is by reason of redundancy, subsequently endorsed by the House of Lords in Murray v Foyle Meats.
leadingCases.significance: Established the analytical framework subsequently adopted by the House of Lords. The three-stage test remains the standard approach in all redundancy cases.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Managing redundancy for employers
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on fair redundancy processes including the statutory definition, consultation requirements, selection criteria, and alternative employment obligations. Not a statutory code but widely referenced by tribunals as a benchmark for reasonableness.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Redundancy: your rights
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on statutory redundancy payments, notice periods, consultation rights, and how to challenge a redundancy. Aimed at employees but sets out the employer's legal obligations clearly.

commonErrors: Applying a 'contract test' (what was the employee contractually employed to do?) rather than the statutory 'work of a particular kind' test endorsed in Murray v Foyle Meats
Assuming that because a role has been retitled or restructured, no redundancy has occurred — the test is whether the requirement for work of a particular kind has diminished
Failing to recognise that an employer who replaces an employee with a cheaper alternative is not making a genuine redundancy — this may be a 'sham redundancy'

sections.title: Statutory redundancy payment calculation
sections.summary: Employees with at least two years' continuous service are entitled to a statutory redundancy payment calculated by reference to age, length of service, and a week's pay (subject to a statutory cap). The formula provides: 0.5 week's pay for each complete year of service aged under 22; 1 week's pay for each year aged 22–40; 1.5 weeks' pay for each year aged 41 or over. Maximum 20 years' service counts.
sections.text: Section 162 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 sets out the calculation. The amount of a redundancy payment shall be calculated by reference to the period, ending with the relevant date, during which the employee has been continuously employed, by starting at the end of that period and reckoning backwards the number of years of employment falling within that period, and allowing the appropriate amount for each of those years.
refs: ERA 1996 s.162 (calculation of redundancy payment)
ERA 1996 s.227 (a week's pay limit — currently £643 from April 2024)
ERA 1996 s.155 (qualifying period — 2 years)
ERA 1996 s.135 (entitlement to redundancy payment)
ERA 1999 s.34 (indexation of limits)
commonErrors: Using gross pay rather than the statutory week's pay cap — the statutory redundancy payment is calculated using the LOWER of the employee's actual week's pay or the statutory cap (currently £643). Many employers confuse this with the employee's actual salary

Counting more than 20 years of service — the statutory formula counts backwards from the relevant date and only includes the last 20 complete years of continuous service. Service beyond 20 years is disregarded

Not distinguishing between statutory redundancy pay (tax-free up to £30,000) and enhanced contractual redundancy pay — the tax treatment depends on whether the contractual payment exceeds the statutory entitlement. ITEPA 2003 s.403 provides the £30,000 exemption for qualifying termination payments

Failing to calculate variable hours correctly — for employees with variable hours, a week's pay is calculated as the average over the 12 weeks ending with the calculation date (ERA 1996 s.224). Weeks in which no pay was earned are excluded and replaced by earlier weeks

Overlooking that enhanced contractual redundancy payments may create expectations that bind the employer — if an employer has consistently paid enhanced redundancy above the statutory minimum, this may become an implied contractual term through custom and practice
Not recognising that employees who are laid off or put on short-time working for an extended period may claim a statutory redundancy payment under ERA 1996 ss.147–154, even without a formal dismissal

sections.title: Trial period for alternative employment
sections.summary: Where an employee under notice of redundancy is offered alternative employment that takes effect within four weeks of the original contract ending, the employee has a statutory trial period of four weeks in the new role. If either party terminates the contract during the trial period for a reason connected with the change, the employee is treated as having been dismissed by reason of redundancy at the date the original contract ended, preserving their right to a redundancy payment. The trial period may be extended by written agreement for the purpose of retraining.
sections.text: Section 138 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 provides at subsection (1) that where an employee's contract is renewed, or the employee is re-engaged under a new contract, in circumstances where the renewal or re-engagement takes effect on the ending of the old contract or within four weeks, the employee is not regarded as dismissed for redundancy payment purposes. Subsection (2) provides that if the terms of the new contract differ (wholly or in part) from the corresponding provisions of the old contract, the employee is entitled to a trial period of four weeks starting from the date the new contract takes effect. Subsection (3) provides that if the employee, for whatever reason, terminates or gives notice to terminate the new contract during the trial period, or the employer terminates it for a reason connected with or arising out of the change, the employee is to be treated as having been dismissed on the date the old contract ended.
sections.keyPrinciple: The statutory trial period is a protective mechanism for employees who accept alternative roles — it allows them to try the new position without losing their right to a redundancy payment if the role proves unsuitable. The trial period can be extended by written agreement solely for retraining purposes (s.138(3)(b)).
refs: ERA 1996 s.138 (renewal of contract or re-engagement)
ERA 1996 s.141 (employee refusing or unreasonably terminating alternative employment loses redundancy payment)
Elliot v Richard Stump Ltd [1987] ICR 579 (EAT) — trial period starts on first day of new employment
Optical Express Ltd v Williams [2007] IRLR 936 (EAT) — extension of trial period must be in writing before the original period expires
commonErrors: Not putting the trial period extension agreement in writing before the original 4-week period expires — an oral extension is void
Failing to recognise that the trial period applies automatically where the terms of the new contract differ from the old — no agreement is needed to invoke it

Dismissing the employee during the trial period for a reason unconnected with the change in employment — this breaks the chain and the employee is treated as dismissed under the new contract, not by reason of redundancy

sections.title: Unreasonable refusal of suitable alternative employment
sections.summary: An employee who unreasonably refuses an offer of suitable alternative employment, or who unreasonably terminates the new contract during the trial period, loses their right to a statutory redundancy payment. The questions of whether the alternative is 'suitable' and whether the refusal is 'unreasonable' are distinct — suitability is assessed objectively by reference to the terms and nature of the role, while reasonableness of refusal is assessed subjectively from the employee's perspective.
sections.text: Section 141 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 provides at subsection (1) that an employee is not entitled to a redundancy payment if, before the end of the old contract, the employer or an associated employer offers to renew the contract or re-engage the employee under a new contract on the same terms, and the employee unreasonably refuses. Subsection (2) extends this to offers of suitable employment on different terms. Subsection (4) provides that where the employee is entitled to a trial period under s.138 and unreasonably terminates the contract during that period, the employee is not entitled to a redundancy payment.
sections.keyPrinciple: Suitability and reasonableness are separate tests. An offer may be objectively suitable but the employee's refusal may be reasonable (e.g., personal circumstances, family commitments, health reasons). Conversely, an offer may be less than ideally suitable but the employee's refusal may be unreasonable if no better alternative exists.

leadingCases.name: Cambridge and District Co-operative Society Ltd v Ruse
leadingCases.principle: The suitability of alternative employment is assessed by reference to the terms and nature of the role (an objective test). The reasonableness of the employee's refusal is assessed from the employee's perspective (a subjective test), taking into account all their personal circumstances.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed the two-stage test: first, was the alternative employment 'suitable' — assessed objectively by comparing the terms, status, and nature of the work with the original role? Second, was the employee's refusal 'unreasonable' — assessed subjectively by reference to the employee's personal circumstances, including domestic, family, and health factors?
leadingCases.significance: Established the distinction between the objective suitability test and the subjective reasonableness test. The employee's personal circumstances are relevant only to the second question (reasonableness of refusal), not to the first (suitability of the offer).

refs: ERA 1996 s.141 (refusal of suitable alternative employment)
ERA 1996 s.138 (trial period provisions — interact with s.141)
Taylor v Kent County Council [1969] 2 QB 560 — offer of lower-status role may not be 'suitable'

sections.title: Time off to look for work or arrange training
sections.summary: An employee who has been given notice of dismissal by reason of redundancy and has at least two years' continuous service is entitled to reasonable paid time off during working hours to look for new employment or to arrange training for future employment. The maximum entitlement is two-fifths of a week's pay, regardless of the amount of time actually taken.
sections.text:
Section 52(1) of the Employment Rights Act 1996 provides: An employee who is given notice of dismissal by reason of redundancy is entitled to be permitted by his employer to take reasonable time off during the employee's working hours before the end of his notice in order to—

(a) look for new employment, or

(b) make arrangements for training for future employment.

Section 52(2) provides that an employee is only entitled under this section if, on the date on which the notice is due to expire, the employee will have been continuously employed for a period of two years or more.

Section 53(4) provides that the amount of the payment to which the employee is entitled is two-fifths of a week's pay.
sections.keyPrinciple: The right to time off is during working hours, is paid, and does not require the employee to prove they have a specific interview or training appointment — 'looking for new employment' is sufficient. The employer cannot require proof of interviews.
refs: ERA 1996 s.52 (right to time off to look for work)
ERA 1996 s.53 (right to remuneration for time off under s.52)
ERA 1996 s.54 (complaint to employment tribunal for breach of s.52/53)
commonErrors: Not knowing this right exists — many employers are unaware of the statutory right to paid time off during the notice period
Requiring the employee to produce evidence of interviews before granting time off — the statute does not require this
Assuming the employee is entitled to unlimited paid time off — the maximum payment is two-fifths of a week's pay regardless of time taken

sections.title: Redundancy as a potentially fair reason for dismissal
sections.summary: Redundancy is a potentially fair reason for dismissal under ERA 1996 s.98(2)(c). However, even where a genuine redundancy situation exists, the dismissal is only fair if the employer acted reasonably in treating redundancy as a sufficient reason for dismissing the particular employee — the s.98(4) reasonableness test. This requires a fair selection process, adequate consultation with the affected employee, and a genuine search for suitable alternative employment.
sections.text: Section 98 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 provides at subsection (1) that it is for the employer to show the reason for the dismissal. At subsection (2)(c), redundancy is identified as a potentially fair reason. At subsection (4), the tribunal must determine whether the employer acted reasonably in treating the reason as a sufficient reason for dismissing the employee, having regard to equity and the substantial merits of the case.
sections.keyPrinciple: A genuine redundancy does not, by itself, make the dismissal fair. The employer must also demonstrate that: (1) the selection criteria were objectively justified and fairly applied; (2) the employee was individually consulted before selection was finalised; and (3) the employer made reasonable efforts to find suitable alternative employment within the organisation.
refs: ERA 1996 s.98(1)–(4) (fairness of dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.105 (automatically unfair selection for redundancy)
Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd [1987] UKHL 8 — procedural fairness in redundancy
Williams v Compair Maxam Ltd [1982] ICR 156 (EAT) — guidelines for fair redundancy
British Aerospace plc v Green [1995] ICR 1006 (CA) — selection criteria scrutiny

leadingCases.name: Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd
leadingCases.principle: An employer who fails to follow fair redundancy procedures (consultation, selection criteria, alternative employment search) cannot argue that the outcome would have been the same regardless — procedural fairness is essential to the reasonableness of the dismissal.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that an employer who omits consultation or fails to follow a fair selection procedure will normally have acted unreasonably, regardless of whether following the procedure would have made any difference to the outcome. The procedural steps are part of the reasonableness test, not separate from it.
leadingCases.significance: The foundational authority on procedural fairness in redundancy dismissals. Established the 'Polkey reduction' — where the tribunal finds the dismissal unfair for procedural reasons, compensation may be reduced to reflect the percentage chance that the employee would have been dismissed in any event had a fair procedure been followed.

leadingCases.name: Williams v Compair Maxam Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The EAT established guidelines for a reasonable employer carrying out redundancy dismissals: (1) give maximum warning; (2) consult the union (if recognised) about selection criteria; (3) ensure criteria are objective and can be checked; (4) ensure selection is in accordance with the criteria; (5) consider alternative employment.
leadingCases.heldText: Browne-Wilkinson J set out five principles which a reasonable employer would be expected to follow in conducting redundancy dismissals. While not a rigid checklist, departure from these principles without good reason will typically render the dismissal unfair.
leadingCases.significance: The seminal EAT authority on the standards expected of a reasonable employer in redundancy. The five principles remain the starting point for all redundancy fairness assessments.

commonErrors: Treating redundancy as automatically fair — a genuine redundancy situation is only the starting point; the employer must also demonstrate procedural fairness
Applying subjective or untested selection criteria — criteria must be objective, measurable, and capable of independent verification
Failing to conduct meaningful individual consultation before finalising selection — consultation after the decision is made is not genuine consultation
Not searching for suitable alternative employment across the entire organisation, including associated companies

sections.title: Automatically unfair selection for redundancy
sections.summary: Section 105 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 provides that selection for redundancy on certain specified grounds is automatically unfair, regardless of whether the redundancy situation is genuine. These grounds include trade union membership or activities, pregnancy or maternity, exercising statutory rights, whistleblowing, and acting as an employee representative. No qualifying period is required — the protection is from day one.
sections.text:
Section 105 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 provides that an employee who is dismissed shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the reason or principal reason for which the employee was selected for redundancy was one of the automatically unfair reasons specified in the section. The full list of prohibited selection reasons cross-references the following provisions:

s.99 — pregnancy, childbirth, maternity leave, adoption leave, shared parental leave, parental bereavement leave
s.100 — health and safety activities (safety representatives, raising concerns)
s.101 — refusing to work on Sundays (shop/betting workers)
s.101A — working time rights (refusing to exceed 48-hour limit, asserting rest break entitlements)
s.102 — pension scheme trustee activities
s.103 — employee representative activities (consultation representatives)
s.103A — making a protected disclosure (whistleblowing)
s.104 — asserting a statutory right
s.104A — trade union recognition activities (TULRCA Schedule A1)
s.104B — tax credits
s.104C — flexible working requests
s.104D — study and training requests
s.104E — jury service
s.104F — employee shareholder status
TULRCA 1992 s.152–153 — trade union membership, activities, or use of union services
sections.keyPrinciple: If the reason or principal reason for selecting an employee for redundancy is any of the automatically unfair reasons, the dismissal is automatically unfair regardless of whether the redundancy is genuine, the selection criteria were objectively fair, or the employer followed a fair procedure. No range of reasonable responses defence is available — the dismissal is simply unfair. No qualifying period is required for any of these claims.
refs: ERA 1996 s.105 (automatically unfair selection for redundancy)
ERA 1996 ss.99–104F (the automatically unfair reasons cross-referenced by s.105)
TULRCA 1992 ss.152–153 (trade union reasons — automatically unfair)
ERA 1996 s.108(3) (qualifying period disapplied for automatically unfair reasons)
O'Dea v ISC Chemicals Ltd [1995] IRLR 599 (CA) — burden of proof in automatic unfairness claims
commonErrors: Assuming that a genuine redundancy situation immunises the employer from automatically unfair dismissal claims — it does not. Even where the redundancy is genuine and the criteria are objectively fair, if the REASON the particular employee was selected was one of the prohibited reasons, the dismissal is automatically unfair

Not recognising the breadth of the automatically unfair reasons — there are over 15 separate categories spanning trade union activities, pregnancy/maternity, whistleblowing, working time, health and safety, flexible working, and statutory rights assertion

Using selection criteria that are proxies for protected reasons — for example, selecting based on 'flexibility to work overtime' where the employee has refused overtime on working time grounds (s.101A), or based on 'absence record' where the employee was absent on maternity leave (s.99)

Overlooking that employee representative status (s.103) protects employees elected or appointed as consultation representatives during the redundancy process itself — selecting a consultation representative for redundancy because of their representative role is automatically unfair

Failing to understand the burden of proof — where the employee establishes that there is a real issue whether the reason for selection was an automatically unfair reason, the burden shifts to the employer to prove that the selection was for a different reason

sections.title: Bumping — selecting a different employee for redundancy
sections.summary: Bumping occurs where an employer, instead of dismissing the employee whose role is redundant, dismisses a different employee in a different role and moves the first employee into that role. Tribunals have held that a reasonable employer may be expected to consider bumping as part of a fair redundancy process, but there is no absolute obligation to do so — it is one factor in the overall reasonableness assessment.
sections.text: There is no specific statutory provision for bumping. The concept arises from the application of the s.98(4) reasonableness test in the context of alternative employment obligations. The leading authorities establish that a reasonable employer should at least consider whether bumping is a viable option, even if they ultimately decide against it.
sections.keyPrinciple: A failure to consider bumping does not automatically render a redundancy dismissal unfair, but it is a relevant factor in the overall assessment of reasonableness. The tribunal will consider the size and administrative resources of the employer, the practicability of the arrangement, and whether it was raised during consultation.

leadingCases.name: Mirab v Mentor Graphics (UK) Ltd
leadingCases.principle: A reasonable employer is not obliged to bump, but must at least consider it. The failure to consider bumping at all may render the dismissal unfair.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that while there is no absolute duty to bump, a reasonable employer should consider whether bumping is a viable option as part of the search for alternative employment. The obligation is to consider, not to implement — but a complete failure to turn one's mind to the possibility may fall outside the range of reasonable responses.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified that the duty in relation to bumping is a duty to consider, not a duty to implement. Confirmed that bumping remains a relevant factor in the s.98(4) reasonableness assessment.

refs: ERA 1996 s.98(4) (reasonableness of dismissal — bumping assessed under this section)
Vokes Ltd v Bear [1974] ICR 1 (NIRC) — early authority on bumping
Mirab v Mentor Graphics (UK) Ltd [2018] UKEAT/0172/17

sections.title: Voluntary redundancy
sections.summary: There is no statutory framework for voluntary redundancy — it is a contractual arrangement between employer and employee. An employer who invites volunteers is not obliged to accept all volunteers, and an employee who volunteers does not waive their right to claim unfair dismissal if they can show the dismissal was unfair. The distinction between voluntary and compulsory redundancy has significant practical and legal consequences.
sections.text: Voluntary redundancy is not defined in statute. It is a commercially common practice in which the employer invites employees to volunteer for redundancy, typically with enhanced terms. The legal analysis depends on whether the volunteer's departure constitutes a 'dismissal' within ERA 1996 s.136 or a termination by mutual agreement. If the employer initiates the process and sets the terms, it will ordinarily constitute a dismissal (Burton, Allton & Johnson Ltd v Peck [1975] ICR 193).
sections.keyPrinciple: Where an employer opens a voluntary redundancy scheme, accepts a volunteer, and terminates the contract, this is a dismissal by the employer — not a resignation or mutual termination. The employee retains all statutory redundancy and unfair dismissal rights unless validly compromised by a settlement agreement or COT3.

leadingCases.name: Burton, Allton & Johnson Ltd v Peck
leadingCases.principle: Where an employer invites volunteers for redundancy and accepts a volunteer, the termination of the volunteer's employment is a dismissal by the employer, not a resignation or mutual termination by agreement.
leadingCases.heldText: The court held that where an employer, facing a redundancy situation, invited volunteers and accepted the claimant's offer to volunteer, the subsequent termination was a dismissal within the meaning of the statute. The employer initiated the process by creating the voluntary redundancy scheme, set the terms, and decided whether to accept the volunteer. The fact that the employee volunteered did not convert the employer's act of termination into a mutual agreement.
leadingCases.significance: The foundational authority on the legal character of voluntary redundancy. Established that volunteering does not waive statutory rights — the employer still dismisses, and the employee retains the right to a statutory redundancy payment and, potentially, to claim unfair dismissal. This principle has been consistently followed for 50 years.

refs: ERA 1996 s.136 (definition of dismissal — includes expiry of fixed-term contract and employer-initiated termination)
ERA 1996 s.203 (restriction on contracting out — settlement agreement or COT3 required)
Burton, Allton & Johnson Ltd v Peck [1975] ICR 193 — voluntary redundancy is still a dismissal
Scott v Coalite Fuels and Chemicals Ltd [1988] ICR 355 (EAT) — employee who volunteers may still claim unfair dismissal if the process was unfair
commonErrors: Assuming that a volunteer for redundancy cannot claim unfair dismissal — they can, unless the claim is validly compromised by a settlement agreement or COT3
Treating voluntary redundancy as a mutual termination rather than a dismissal — where the employer initiates and controls the process, it is a dismissal
Failing to apply fair selection criteria to volunteers where there are more volunteers than required — the employer must still act reasonably
Not offering the voluntary scheme to all employees in the pool — selective offers may be discriminatory

sections.title: Collective consultation obligation (20+ employees)
sections.summary: Where an employer proposes to dismiss as redundant 20 or more employees at one establishment within a period of 90 days or less, the employer must consult with appropriate representatives of the affected employees. Consultation must begin in good time and, where 100 or more redundancies are proposed, at least 45 days before the first dismissal takes effect. Where 20–99 redundancies are proposed, consultation must begin at least 30 days before. Failure to comply attracts a protective award of up to 90 days' pay per affected employee.
sections.text: Section 188(1) of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 provides: Where an employer is proposing to dismiss as redundant 20 or more employees at one establishment within a period of 90 days or less, the employer shall consult about the dismissals all the persons who are appropriate representatives of any of the employees who may be affected by the proposed dismissals or may be affected by measures taken in connection with those dismissals.
sections.keyPrinciple: Collective consultation is a legal obligation triggered by the number of proposed redundancies at a single establishment — it applies regardless of whether the employer recognises a trade union. The duty is to consult 'in good time' and with a view to reaching agreement about ways of avoiding dismissals, reducing numbers, and mitigating consequences.
refs: TULRCA 1992 s.188 (duty to consult)
TULRCA 1992 s.189 (complaint to employment tribunal — protective award)
TULRCA 1992 s.193 (duty to notify the Secretary of State — HR1 form)
TULRCA 1992 s.195 (meaning of 'redundancy' for collective consultation purposes — broader than ERA 1996 s.139)
ERA 2025 s.70 (amendments to collective consultation — not yet in force)
USDAW v Ethel Austin Ltd (in administration) [2015] ICR 675 (CJEU/UKSC) — 'establishment' meaning
Junk v Kühnel (C-188/03) [2005] ECR I-885 (CJEU) — consultation must precede notice of dismissal

leadingCases.name: USDAW v Ethel Austin Ltd (in administration)
leadingCases.principle: The word 'establishment' in TULRCA 1992 s.188 means the local unit of employment (e.g., a single store), not the entire undertaking. The 20-employee threshold is applied per establishment.
leadingCases.heldText: Following the CJEU preliminary ruling, the Supreme Court held that the duty to collectively consult is triggered when 20 or more redundancies are proposed at a single establishment (local employment unit), not across the entire business. This means employers with multiple small sites may avoid the collective consultation duty even if the total redundancies across all sites exceed 20.
leadingCases.significance: The definitive UKSC authority on the meaning of 'establishment' for collective consultation purposes. This interpretation significantly narrows the scope of the collective consultation duty for multi-site employers.

commonErrors: Counting the 20-employee threshold across the whole organisation rather than at each establishment separately
Issuing individual notice of dismissal before commencing collective consultation — Junk v Kühnel requires consultation to precede notice
Failing to notify the Secretary of State via the HR1 form at least 30 days (or 45 days for 100+) before the first dismissal — a criminal offence under TULRCA 1992 s.194
Using the narrow ERA 1996 s.139 definition of redundancy for the 20-employee count — TULRCA 1992 s.195 uses a broader definition covering all dismissals 'not related to the individual'

sections.title: Secretary of State notification — the HR1 form
sections.summary: In addition to consulting with employee representatives, an employer proposing 20 or more redundancies at one establishment must notify the Secretary of State (now via the Insolvency Service) using the HR1 form. This is a separate obligation from the duty to consult. The notification must be given at least 30 days before the first dismissal takes effect (20–99 redundancies) or at least 45 days before (100+ redundancies). Failure to notify is a criminal offence punishable by an unlimited fine.
sections.text:
Section 193 of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 provides at subsection (1) that an employer proposing to dismiss as redundant 100 or more employees at one establishment within a period of 90 days or less shall give to the Secretary of State, at least 45 days before the first dismissal takes effect, written notice of his proposal. Subsection (2) provides that an employer proposing to dismiss as redundant 20 or more employees at one establishment within such a period shall give at least 30 days' notice.

Section 194 provides at subsection (1) that an employer who fails to give notice under section 193 commits an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a fine.
sections.keyPrinciple: The HR1 notification is a criminal obligation, separate from the civil duty to consult. Failure is a criminal offence carrying an unlimited fine. A copy of the HR1 must also be sent to the representatives of the affected employees.
refs: TULRCA 1992 s.193 (duty to notify Secretary of State)
TULRCA 1992 s.194 (offence of failing to notify)
Insolvency Service — HR1 form submission
TULRCA 1992 s.193(6) (copy to employee representatives)
commonErrors: Not knowing this obligation exists at all — many employers are aware of the consultation duty but not the separate notification duty
Failing to send a copy of the HR1 to the employee representatives — s.193(6) requires this

Not recognising that the time periods (30/45 days) for notification and consultation run concurrently but are independent obligations — failing to notify does not invalidate the consultation, but it is a separate criminal offence

sections.title: The critical distinction: s.139 redundancy vs s.195 'redundancy'
sections.summary: The definition of 'redundancy' for collective consultation purposes (TULRCA 1992 s.195) is significantly broader than the definition for individual redundancy payment purposes (ERA 1996 s.139). Section 195 defines redundancy as 'dismissal for a reason not related to the individual concerned' — which captures not only genuine redundancies but also reorganisations, restructurings, and dismissals for business-efficiency reasons that do not meet the s.139 test. An employer may have 15 'genuine' redundancies under s.139 but 25 'dismissals not related to the individual' under s.195, triggering the collective consultation obligation.
sections.text:
Section 195 of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 provides at subsection (1): In this Chapter references to being 'dismissed as redundant' are references to being dismissed for a reason not related to the individual concerned or for a number of reasons all of which are not so related.

This is fundamentally broader than section 139 of the Employment Rights Act 1996, which requires either cessation of business or diminution in the employer's requirement for employees to carry out work of a particular kind.
sections.keyPrinciple: The s.195 definition catches ALL dismissals 'not related to the individual' — including reorganisations where no roles have disappeared, efficiency dismissals, and restructurings where the overall headcount is unchanged but roles are being redrawn. Many employers are caught by the collective consultation duty because they count only 'genuine redundancies' under s.139 instead of all qualifying dismissals under s.195.
refs: TULRCA 1992 s.195 (meaning of 'redundancy' for collective consultation purposes)
ERA 1996 s.139 (meaning of 'redundancy' for individual payment purposes)
Hardy v Tourism South East [2005] IRLR 242 (EAT) — confirmed the breadth of the s.195 definition
Optare Group Ltd v TGWU [2007] IRLR 931 (EAT) — business reorganisation dismissals fall within s.195
commonErrors: Using the s.139 definition to count the number of proposed redundancies for the collective consultation threshold — this underestimates the true count and may result in the employer failing to collectively consult when the s.195 threshold is exceeded

Assuming that a restructuring exercise where roles are being redrawn (rather than eliminated) does not trigger collective consultation — it does, because the dismissals are 'not related to the individual'
Failing to include the expiry of fixed-term contracts in the count — where the employer decides not to renew fixed-term contracts as part of a broader programme, these are dismissals under s.195

sections.title: Discrimination in redundancy selection
sections.summary: Selection criteria and the application of those criteria must not directly or indirectly discriminate against employees on grounds of any protected characteristic (age, disability, gender reassignment, marriage/civil partnership, pregnancy/maternity, race, religion/belief, sex, sexual orientation). Selection criteria that appear neutral may be indirectly discriminatory if they disproportionately disadvantage a protected group and cannot be objectively justified.
sections.text: Section 13 of the Equality Act 2010 (direct discrimination) and section 19 (indirect discrimination) apply to all aspects of the redundancy process including selection criteria design, application of criteria, consultation procedures, and offers of alternative employment.
sections.keyPrinciple: Redundancy selection criteria such as 'last in, first out' (LIFO), absence records, performance scores, and flexibility must be assessed for both direct and indirect discriminatory impact against all protected characteristics.
refs: EqA 2010 s.13 (direct discrimination)
EqA 2010 s.19 (indirect discrimination)
EqA 2010 s.18 (pregnancy/maternity discrimination)
EqA 2010 s.15 (discrimination arising from disability)
EqA 2010 s.20 (duty to make reasonable adjustments)
EHRC Employment Code of Practice para 9.25–9.35

leadingCases.name: Rolls-Royce plc v Unite the Union
leadingCases.principle: Length of service as ONE factor in a redundancy selection matrix is capable of being objectively justified as indirect age discrimination — but using length of service (LIFO) as the sole criterion would almost certainly be unlawful.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that including length of service as one of six scored criteria in a collectively agreed redundancy selection matrix constituted indirect age discrimination against younger workers, but was justified as a proportionate means of achieving legitimate aims — specifically, rewarding loyalty, protecting older workers who would find it harder to obtain alternative employment, and facilitating peaceful redundancy processes. The court distinguished this from 'last in first out' (LIFO) as a sole criterion, which would be 'objectionable'.
leadingCases.significance: The leading EWCA authority on length of service in redundancy selection and age discrimination. Established the principle that service-based criteria can survive indirect discrimination challenges when used as part of a balanced, multi-factor matrix. Effectively ended the use of pure LIFO in sophisticated employers while preserving service as a legitimate component of selection. The case also confirmed that collectively agreed criteria carry additional weight in the justification analysis.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Employment Statutory Code of Practice
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Paragraphs 9.25–9.35 address redundancy selection and discrimination. The Code confirms that selection criteria must be checked for disparate impact against all protected characteristics, and that absence-based criteria must discount disability-related absence.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Handling redundancy
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on fair selection criteria, consultation procedures, and alternative employment offers. Not a statutory code but widely used as a benchmark for tribunal assessments of reasonableness.

commonErrors: Using absence records as a selection criterion without discounting disability-related absences — this may constitute discrimination arising from disability (EqA 2010 s.15) unless objectively justified
Using 'flexibility' or 'willingness to work overtime' as criteria without considering the disproportionate impact on employees with childcare responsibilities (indirect sex discrimination)
Applying LIFO without recognising that it may indirectly discriminate against younger workers or recently returned maternity leave employees
Failing to make reasonable adjustments to the selection process for disabled employees (EqA 2010 s.20)

parts.title: Stage 5 — Enhanced Protection: Pregnancy and Family Leave
parts.instrumentSource: MPL 1999 · Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 · ERA 2025 ss.14–41

sections.title: Priority right to suitable alternative employment
sections.summary: Where an employee is on maternity leave, adoption leave, or shared parental leave, and their position becomes redundant, Regulation 10 of the Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 requires the employer to offer any suitable available vacancy on terms no less favourable. The Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023, which came into force on 6 April 2024, extends this priority right to cover the period of pregnancy (before maternity leave starts) and a protected period after return from leave.
sections.text: Regulation 10(1) of the Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 provides: Where during an employee's ordinary or additional maternity leave period it is not practicable by reason of redundancy for her employer to continue to employ her under her existing contract of employment, she is entitled to be offered (before the end of her employment under that contract) alternative employment with her employer.
sections.keyPrinciple: Employees on maternity leave, adoption leave, or shared parental leave have a priority right to be offered suitable alternative vacancies ahead of other redundant employees. This is not a right to be immune from redundancy — it is a right to preferential consideration for alternative roles.
refs: MPL 1999 Reg.10 (right to be offered suitable alternative employment during maternity leave)
Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 (extends Reg.10 priority to pregnancy and post-leave period)
Maternity and Parental Leave etc. (Amendment) Regulations 2024 (SI 2024/327) — implementing regulations
ERA 1996 s.99 (automatically unfair dismissal for pregnancy/maternity reasons)
EqA 2010 s.18 (pregnancy and maternity discrimination)
ERA 2025 ss.14–41 (further strengthened protections — not yet in force)
sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 sections 14–41 contain significant further strengthening of redundancy protection during pregnancy and family leave. These provisions are enacted but not yet in force as at March 2026. When commenced, they will extend the protected period, strengthen the priority right mechanism, and increase the remedies available for breach.

leadingCases.name: Sefton Borough Council v Wainwright
leadingCases.principle: The duty to offer a suitable alternative vacancy under Regulation 10 arises when the employer becomes aware that the employee's role is redundant or potentially redundant — not when the restructuring exercise is complete. The employee on maternity leave must be offered the vacancy, not merely given the opportunity to apply for it in competition with others.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the Council breached Regulation 10 by requiring an employee on maternity leave to compete for a new combined role through a competitive interview process rather than offering her the role directly. The duty is an absolute right: where a suitable alternative vacancy exists, it must be offered to the employee on maternity leave — it is not for the employer to assess the employee's suitability through a competitive process. However, the EAT overturned the finding of direct pregnancy/maternity discrimination — a breach of Regulation 10 does not automatically constitute discrimination under EqA 2010 s.18; the tribunal must separately consider whether the reason for the unfavourable treatment was the pregnancy or maternity leave.
leadingCases.significance: The leading EAT authority on the timing and nature of the Regulation 10 duty. Established three critical points: (1) the duty arises when the employer first knows the role is at risk, not after the restructuring is complete; (2) the duty requires an offer, not merely an opportunity to compete; (3) a breach of Regulation 10 is automatically unfair dismissal but is not automatically pregnancy discrimination — the two claims require separate analysis.

leadingCases.name: Community Task Force v Rimmer
leadingCases.principle: The test of availability of a suitable alternative vacancy is not qualified by considerations of what is economic or reasonable. If a suitable vacancy is available, the consequences of giving the job to the protected employee are not relevant.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the question of availability is a factual one — the tribunal must simply ask whether a suitable vacancy exists and is available. If it is, the employer must offer it regardless of practical or economic consequences.
leadingCases.significance: Established the absolute nature of the Regulation 10 right — suitability is assessed on the facts, but once a vacancy is found to be suitable and available, economic or practical arguments against offering it to the protected employee are irrelevant.

commonErrors: Treating the Reg.10 priority as a right to retain the existing role — it is a right to preferential offer of a suitable alternative vacancy, not immunity from redundancy
Failing to identify ALL suitable vacancies across the entire organisation (and associated companies) before concluding that no alternative employment exists
Not recognising that the Protection from Redundancy Act 2023 extends the protected period beyond the maternity leave itself — it now covers pregnancy and a period after return

Overlooking that a failure to offer a suitable alternative vacancy to a pregnant/maternity leave employee, when such a vacancy was offered to a non-pregnant employee, will almost certainly constitute pregnancy discrimination under EqA 2010 s.18

parts.title: Stage 6 — Redundancy in a TUPE Transfer Context

sections.title: Redundancy dismissals connected with a transfer
sections.summary: Regulation 7 of TUPE 2006 provides that a dismissal connected with a relevant transfer is automatically unfair unless the employer can show that the reason for the dismissal was an economic, technical, or organisational reason entailing changes in the workforce (an 'ETO reason'). Where an ETO reason is established, the dismissal is treated as a redundancy for the purposes of ERA 1996 and must still satisfy the s.98(4) reasonableness test.
sections.text: Regulation 7(1) of TUPE 2006 provides: Where either before or after a relevant transfer, any employee of the transferor or transferee is dismissed, that employee shall be treated for the purposes of Part X of the 1996 Act as unfairly dismissed if the sole or principal reason for his dismissal is the transfer.
sections.keyPrinciple: A dismissal is automatically unfair if the sole or principal reason is the TUPE transfer itself. It is only potentially fair if the employer can establish an ETO reason entailing changes in the workforce — and even then, the dismissal must satisfy the ordinary unfair dismissal fairness test.
refs: TUPE 2006 Reg.7 (dismissal because of relevant transfer)
TUPE 2006 Reg.4 (automatic transfer of employment contracts)
ERA 1996 s.98(4) (reasonableness test — applied to ETO dismissals)
Berriman v Delabole Slate Ltd [1985] ICR 546 (CA) — 'changes in the workforce' means changes in numbers or functions, not terms
ERA 2025 s.75 (amendments to TUPE — not yet in force)

leadingCases.name: Berriman v Delabole Slate Ltd
leadingCases.principle: An 'economic, technical or organisational reason entailing changes in the workforce' requires changes in the number or functions of the workforce — a change to terms and conditions alone is not sufficient.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that an ETO reason must entail 'changes in the workforce' — meaning changes in the composition, numbers, or functions of the workforce. A desire to harmonise terms and conditions following a transfer does not constitute an ETO reason because it does not entail changes in the workforce itself.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on the meaning of 'entailing changes in the workforce' — establishing that harmonisation of terms is not an ETO reason. This severely limits the circumstances in which a transferee can fairly dismiss transferred employees.

commonErrors: Assuming that redundancies can be carried out before a TUPE transfer to avoid transferring surplus employees — pre-transfer redundancies connected with the transfer are automatically unfair under Reg.7(1)
Confusing an ETO reason (which makes the dismissal potentially fair) with a justification for the transfer itself — they are legally distinct
Failing to recognise that even where an ETO reason is established, the dismissal must still satisfy the ordinary s.98(4) fairness test including consultation and selection

parts.title: Stage 7 — ERA 2025 Forward Intelligence
parts.instrumentSource: Employment Rights Act 2025 (not yet in force)

sections.title: ERA 2025 strengthened redundancy protections
sections.summary: The Employment Rights Act 2025 contains significant provisions that will strengthen redundancy protections when commenced. These include: removal of the unfair dismissal qualifying period (s.1 — making day-one redundancy dismissals subject to the full s.98(4) fairness test); strengthened collective consultation obligations (s.70 — removing the 'single establishment' limitation); and enhanced pregnancy/family leave protections (ss.14–41).
sections.text: ERA 2025 contains multiple provisions affecting redundancy law. These are enacted but not yet fully in force as at March 2026. The Employment Rights Act 2025 (Commencement No. 1 and Transitional and Saving Provisions) Regulations 2026 (SI 2026/3) brought specified provisions into force on 6 January 2026. Further commencement orders are expected throughout 2026 and 2027.

si2026_3.title: Employment Rights Act 2025 (Commencement No. 1 and Transitional and Saving Provisions) Regulations 2026
si2026_3.provisions: Specified provisions including amendments to ERA 1999 s.10(7) (right to be accompanied cross-references), ERA 1999 s.3 blacklisting amendments (separate commencement 18 Feb 2026 by s.159(2)(e)), and various other provisions. Does NOT commence s.1 (removal of qualifying period) or s.70 (collective consultation reform).

awaitingCommencement: s.1 — removal of unfair dismissal qualifying period (CRITICAL for redundancy — all short-service dismissals will carry UD exposure)
s.70 — collective consultation reform (may remove 'single establishment' limitation from USDAW v Ethel Austin)
ss.14–41 — enhanced pregnancy/family leave protections during redundancy
commencementTracking.governmentTimetable: The Government's stated intention is phased commencement through 2026–2027. No confirmed dates for the major provisions (s.1, s.70, ss.14–41). Employers should begin preparation before commencement is confirmed — retrofitting compliance after commencement will be significantly more expensive and risky.

sections.keyPrinciple: ERA 2025 represents the most significant reform of redundancy law since the Employment Relations Act 1999. Employers should begin preparing for the changes before commencement.
sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 s.1 (removal of qualifying period): When commenced, every employee dismissed by reason of redundancy from day one of employment will have the right to claim unfair dismissal. This transforms the redundancy landscape — employers will no longer be able to rely on the two-year qualifying period as a barrier to unfair dismissal claims for short-service employees. ERA 2025 s.70 (collective consultation): Proposes to remove or amend the 'single establishment' limitation established in USDAW v Ethel Austin, potentially requiring collective consultation where 20+ redundancies are proposed across the entire undertaking, not just at one establishment.
refs: ERA 2025 s.1 (removal of qualifying period for unfair dismissal)
ERA 2025 s.70 (amendments to collective consultation obligations)
ERA 2025 ss.14–41 (enhanced pregnancy and family leave protections)
ERA 2025 s.75 (amendments to TUPE)
ERA 1996 s.108 (qualifying period — to be removed by ERA 2025 s.1)
commonErrors: Assuming ERA 2025 is not yet relevant because the major provisions are not in force — some provisions commenced 6 January 2026 (SI 2026/3) and employers should already be reviewing their redundancy processes in anticipation of the remaining provisions

Not preparing for the removal of the qualifying period (s.1) — when commenced, every redundancy dismissal from day one of employment will carry unfair dismissal exposure. Employers who currently rely on the 2-year qualifying period as a buffer for short-service redundancies will face a fundamentally different risk landscape

Overlooking the potential reform of collective consultation thresholds (s.70) — if the 'single establishment' limitation is removed, multi-site employers who currently avoid collective consultation by making fewer than 20 redundancies at each site will need to count redundancies across the entire undertaking

Failing to document the redundancy process to the standard that will be required post-ERA 2025 — the removal of the qualifying period means tribunals will scrutinise the fairness of short-service redundancy dismissals that were previously outside their jurisdiction
